coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of photoelectric performance for CsPbI2Br solar cells by the synergistic effect of binary additives

coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qiulu
Lin, Like
Wang, Yanzhou
Gao, Zhe
Fu, Yujun
Liu, Qiming
Li, Junshuai
He, Deyan
coredata.dc:description: CsPbI2Br-based perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have attracted much attention because of their excellent phase stability and appropriate bandgap. However, numerous defects of undercoordinated ions or mobile species are the sites of carrier nonradiative recombination, causing a low power conversion efficiency (PCE). In this work, NaCl and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as binary additives are introduced into perovskite precursor to obtain high-quality photoactive films. Chloride ion (Cl−) is incorporated into perovskite due to the same physical and chemical properties as bromine (Br−), that align the energy level of CsPbI2Br, decrease the energy barrier between perovskite and P3HT to promote carrier transport and extraction, hence result in the reduced energy loss. Meanwhile, because of its good conductivity, N-GQDs at grain boundaries can rapidly conduct photogenerated electrons to SnO2, suppressing carrier recombination at grain boundaries. Furthermore, the trap state density of the CsPbI2Br film with binary additives is reduced, which could prolong the carrier lifetime, and improve surface morphology. As a result, a PCE of 15.37% for CsPbI2Br PSCs with binary additives is obtained，which shows ∼22.76% relative increment compare with the pristine PSCs. Therefore, a simple and convenient optimization strategy of binary additives for PSCs is proposed in this work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: All-inorganic perovskite solar cells
Synergistic effect
Carrier transport
Binary additives engineering

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication CsPbI2Br perovskite solar cells with binary additives

Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Qiming Liu received his BS and MS degrees in Physics from Lanzhou University China, in 2008 and 2011, respectively. After that, he pursued his PhD degrees, under financial support of Japanese Government Scholarship, in Materials Science from Graduate School of Science and Engineering in Saitama University, Japan, in 2015. Then he stayed in Saitama University as a project researcher for two years. Currently, he is an associate professor in institute of electronic materials of Lanzhou University, China. His research focuses on the use of an integrated approach combining materials, interface, device, and process engineering to improve the energy devices especially in the field of photovoltaics and rechargeable batteries.

This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61804070 and 11674138 ), the Science and Technology Projects of Gansu Province (No. 20JR10RA611 and 21JR7RA467 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. lzujbky-2021-60 and lzujbky-2021-sp54 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the interaction between N-GQDs and CsPbI2Br film.

(a) XRD patterns and (b) Comparison of the (100) peaks of CsPbI2Br films with additives. (c) Absorbance spectra and (d) Tauc plot curves for CsPbI2Br films with additives. (e)–(h) Top-view SEM for CsPbI2Br films without and with additives.
(a) Device configuration. (b) J–V curves and (c) Statistical chart of the PCE values. (d) EQE spectra and integrated J sc for CsPbI2Br PSCs.

(a)–(b) UPS spectra for pristine and CsPbI2Br film with NaCl + N-GQDs of the high binding energy (left) region and the low binding energy (right) region. (c)–(d) Energy level alignment of films for pristine PSCs and film with NaCl + N-GQDs PSCs. (e) Carrier transfer and extraction for photoactive layer without and with NaCl + N-GQDs.

(a) Steady-state PL and (b) TRPL spectra of CsPbI2Br film with and without additives deposited on ITO glass. (c) Nyquist plots of the EIS curves at the bias of 1.0 V for the solar cells with and without additives. (d) I–V curves of the electron-only structural devices with and without binary additives in the CsPbI2Br layers.

(a) Normalized PCEs for performance stability of the pristine and with NaCl + N-GQDs PSCs in the air with a humidity of 20%–30 %. (b)–(c) show the water contact angle test of the pristine and CsPbI2Br layers with NaCl + N-GQDs, respectively.
Key device parameters extracted from the J–V curves in Fig. 2(b).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of The Chinese Ceramic Society.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of photoelectric performance for CsPbI2Br solar cells by the synergistic effect of binary additives

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Engineering and Applied Sciences
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Artificial Functional Materials
Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Microstructures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Artificial Functional Materials, Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Microstructures, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210093, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CsPbI2Br-based perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have attracted much attention because of their excellent phase stability and appropriate bandgap. However, numerous defects of undercoordinated ions or mobile species are the sites of carrier nonradiative recombination, causing a low power conversion efficiency (PCE). In this work, NaCl and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as binary additives are introduced into perovskite precursor to obtain high-quality photoactive films. Chloride ion (Cl−) is incorporated into perovskite due to the same physical and chemical properties as bromine (Br−), that align the energy level of CsPbI2Br, decrease the energy barrier between perovskite and P3HT to promote carrier transport and extraction, hence result in the reduced energy loss. Meanwhile, because of its good conductivity, N-GQDs at grain boundaries can rapidly conduct photogenerated electrons to SnO2, suppressing carrier recombination at grain boundaries. Furthermore, the trap state density of the CsPbI2Br film with binary additives is reduced, which could prolong the carrier lifetime, and improve surface morphology. As a result, a PCE of 15.37% for CsPbI2Br PSCs with binary additives is obtained，which shows ∼22.76% relative increment compare with the pristine PSCs. Therefore, a simple and convenient optimization strategy of binary additives for PSCs is proposed in this work.

ce:section.ce:para: All-inorganic perovskite solar cells (PSCs) offer intrinsically thermal stable black perovskite phase over 400 °C and super photostability [1,2], result in a longer service life than organic-inorganic hybrid PSCs. Stirringly, CsPbI3 all-inorganic perovskite has an appropriate band-gap of 1.73 eV [3], brings a lot of research space in PSCs [4–7]. However, the small ion radius of Cs+ harms the perovskite Goldschmidt tolerance factor, leading to the undesirable transition from photoactive black perovskite phase (α-CsPbI3) to non-perovskite yellow phase (δ-CsPbI3) [8]. To overcome this issue, some researches have reported that partially incorporating Br into the CsPbI3 perovskite can adjust the structural stability. CsPbI2Br, stable in the cubic phase at room temperature even for bulk materials, has gained extensive attention [9–13].

In 2016, Snaith et al. first applied CsPbI2Br to solar cells, and a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 9.8% has been observed [14]. In the next few years, CsPbI2Br-based PSCs have attracted extensive attention [15]. Nevertheless, the PCE is much lower than 25% of organic-inorganic hybrid PSCs [2]. This is mainly due to numerous defects of undercoordinated ions or mobile species, which introduce electronic trap states that act an electronic bridge for the charge nonradiative recombination [16]. Therefore, massive energy loss (E loss) accumulates at the defect, making the actual open voltage (V oc) difficult to reach the theoretical value [17–19]. Furthermore, the moisture instability of CsPbI2Br is still an outstanding issue. The moisture adsorbed on the CsPbI2Br surface can introduce vacancies to the crystal lattice, limiting the long-term stability of the solar cells [20,21]. Based on the above problems, many researchers have made much efforts, such as energy level matching, doping density optimizing [22], and charge transport and collection [23]. Among them, additive engineering is a common method to improve the crystallinity and passivate the grain boundaries, thus suppressing the ion migration and carrier recombination [24,25]. Metal chloride additive can decorate the surfaces defects and grain boundaries of perovskites thin film, resulting in the high-quality photoactive layers, fewer defect states, and better surface morphology. Otherwise, doping chloride ions into the perovskite structure can change its energy level to reduce the energy barrier between perovskite and carrier transport layer, finally reduce the E loss at the interface [26–28]. What's more, many kinds of inorganic or organic additive material play an important role in enhancing carrier lifetime, accelerating carrier transport and extraction of perovskite material [29,30]. Due to its excellent crystallinity and tunable bandgap [31], nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) is an ideal additive in PSCs. N-GQDs can realize the migration of electrons from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of GQDs to the conduction band (CB) of the electron transport layer (ETL) by adjusting its band-gap structure, to improve the electron transport ability and hence the performance of photovoltaic device.

Inspired by these works, NaCl and N-GQDs as binary additives were introduced into the perovskite thin film and corresponding PSCs were assembled. The effects of different additives on PSCs performance were studied. N-GQDs intentionally play a substantial role in suppressing the traps density, improving the interface between layers to increase the fill factor (FF) of perovskite devices. Whereas, adding NaCl to CsPbI2Br effectively change the energy band of perovskite, realize a better match with the energy level of P3HT, which is beneficial for transmission and extraction of carriers. The CsPbI2Br film under the synergistic effect of two additives exhibits increased optical performance and improved film quality, results in a PCE of 15.37% with a V oc of 1.24 V and an FF of 78%, versus PCE of 12.52% with a V oc of 1.16 V and an FF of 72% for the control device.

Cesium iodide (CsI), lead bromide (PbBr2), lead iodide (PbI2), and the hole transport material of poly (3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl) (P3HT) were purchased from Xi'an Polymer Light Technology Corp. The electron transport material tin oxide (SnO2) was acquired from Alfa-Aesar. Sodium chloride (NaCl) was bought from Tianjin Guangfu Development Tech. Co., Ltd. N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-QDGs) were got from Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech. Co., Ltd. Chlorobenzene was obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory of Tianjin. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMF) and dimethylimide (DMSO) were purchased from Aladdin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication CsPbI2Br perovskite solar cells with binary additives
ce:section.ce:para: ITO glasses were etched into the corresponding size and then placed in detergent, alcohol, acetone, and isopropanol of ultrasonic cleaning for 15 min, respectively. After dying, the ITO glasses were treated by air plasma for 2.5 min. SnO2 colloidal solution, 15% （in mass） dispersion in water, was diluted in DI water with a volume ratio of 1:4, followed by spin-coating onto the ITO substrate at 3 000 r/min for the 40 s and then thermal annealing at 150 °C for 30 min. For the photoactive perovskite layer, 148 mg PbBr2, 184 mg PbI2, and 208 mg CsI were dissolved in a mixed solution of DMF: DMSO (volume ratio of 4:1), and then stirred in the water bath for 3 h at 90 °C to form the CsPbI2Br precursor with a concentration of 0.8 mol/L. For the devices with N-QDGs, NaCl, and N-QDGs + NaCl, additives are added directly to the precursor solution. After full dissolution, the precursor solution was spin-coated onto the electron transport layer at 3 000 r/min for 60 s in an argon atmosphere, followed by annealing at 50 °C for 30 s to nucleate and crystallize, and at 160 °C for 10 min to enlarge grains. The P3HT was prepared into a solution of 16 mg/mL in chlorobenzene, and then was spin-coating on top of the perovskite layer at 2 000 r/min for 30 s, followed by annealing at 100 °C to form a compact hole transport layer. The 100 nm Ag, as an anode electrode, was thermally evaporated onto the P3HT layer. The density functional theory (DFT) calculations model and the characterization methods can be found in the Supporting Information.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum is obtained to verify organic functional groups in N-GQDs, and shown in Fig. S1. The peaks around 3 300–3 500 cm−1 are attributed to the OH groups, and the absorption at 3 128 cm−1 and 1 433 cm−1 are indexed to NH and CN stretching vibration, respectively. What's more, the CO groups are located at 1 658 and 1 554 cm−1 [32–35]. According to the previous reports, there is strong hydrogen bonding interaction of the OH and NH with the halide ions (I−, Br−) of perovskite components, while CO will interact with free Pb on the CsPbI2Br [36–39]. Therefore, N-GQDs can passivate not only cations but also anions in the CsPbI2Br perovskite, as illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Fig. 2 (a) shows the XRD patterns with and without various additives of perovskite films exhibit similar diffractions peaks of the cubic α-CsPbI2Br [40]. It indicates that the complete cubic structure remains regardless of the introduction of additive. To check the influence of additions on crystallinity, the (100) plane peak was compared before and after incorporation (see Fig. 2(b)). Interestingly, the (100) peak for the films with NaCl and NaCl + N-GQDS shift to the higher 2θ degree, but no shift was found for the films with N-GQDs, which is due to the lattice contraction after Cl− incorporation in the perovskite lattice, while N-GQDs distribute at grain boundaries and/or surfaces. Nevertheless, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) value of the (100) plane exhibits a decreasing trend (FWHM with different additives is plotted in Fig. S2), indicative of improved CsPbI2Br crystallinity. A series of characterizations for the CsPbI2Br layers with varying quantities of N-GQDs and NaCl addition are displayed in Figs. S3–S4. Fig. 2(c) exhibits the UV–Vis absorption spectra of all perovskite samples. One can see that a slight improvement of absorption intensity below 500 nm wavelength is observed, indicates a better crystallinity as well [41]. Notably, the optical band-gap (E opt) estimated from the Tauc plot of (αhv) [2] versus hv (see Fig. 2(d)), which was derived from the corresponding absorption spectra, reveals a values of 1.91 eV for CsPbI2Br [42].

In order to investigate the effects of binary additives on the crystallization process of CsPbI2Br film, photographs of the CsPbI2Br films with and without binary additives were monitored after annealing at 50 °C for different times. As shown in Fig. S5, the variation in film color indicates that the binary additives accelerate the crystallization process of perovskite. This can be ascribed to the introduction of Cl− which may distort the [PbI6]4− octahedral in perovskite crystal, and thus breaks the continuous periodic structure of perovskite at a certain degree [43,44]. This may result in a smaller grain size, which is beneficial to stabilize the black α-phase CsPbI2Br perovskites due to its higher surface energy [28,44]. Next, the surface morphologies of CsPbI2Br films with and without additives are compiled in Fig. 2(e-g). Notably, the top-view SEM image of pristine perovskite film shows nonnegligible holes and deep gullies, while the CsPbI2Br film with N-GQDs exhibits a smooth and compact surface morphology. The addition of NaCl can effectively reduce the existence of a large number of bulk defects. When NaCl and N-GQDs are added to the CsPbI2Br at the same time, the film shows a denser surface and more homogeneous size of the crystalline grain than the pristine one. AFM images (see in Fig. S6) indicate that the surficial root-mean-square (RMS) roughness of the CsPbI2Br film with binary additives (5.56 nm) is significantly lower than that of the pristine film (8.26 nm). In addition, the cross-sectional SEM images for (see Fig. S7) the film without and with NaCl + N-GQDs have a similar thickness of ∼390 nm, which indicates the binary additives have no obvious effect on the thickness of CsPbI2Br film. The high-quality film leads to lower film defects and fewer carrier recombination centers, resulting in an increasing open-circuit voltage (V oc) and short-circuit current density (J sc). Meanwhile, the flat surface leads to better contact with P3HT and less interface energy loss, causing mitigating recombination and better device performance.

PSCs devices were fabricated with a plane configuration of ITO/SnO2/perovskite/P3HT/Ag, and the structural diagram and the cross-sectional SEM image for the devices with and without additive are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and Fig. S8. The current density-voltage (J–V) curves of CsPbI2Br PSCs with and without different additives measured under simulated AM 1.5G illumination are shown in Fig. 3(b). The corresponding photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1 . The J–V curves for solar cells with various concentrations of N-GQDs and NaCl are exhibited in Fig. S9. The pristine PSC display a V oc of 1.16 V, a J sc of 14.93 mA/cm2, a fill factor (FF) of 72%, and a PCE of 12.52%. With NaCl and N-GQDs addition into the perovskite layer, the performance of the solar cell is improved, a PCE of 15.37 % is obtained with a record V oc of 1.24 V, a J sc of 15.89 mA/cm2, and an FF of 78%. Additive engineering improves the comprehensive performance of the devices, especially V oc. To confirm the reliability and repeatability of this improvement, other 9 independent devices with and without NaCl + N-GQDs were fabricated, and the corresponding photovoltaic parameters are counted and exhibited in Fig. 3(c) and Fig. S10 to observe the systematic change trend. The integrated J sc value obtained from the EQE measurement of the devices with and without NaCl + N-GQDs are calculated to be 15.43 and 14.67 mA/cm2, respectively, which are well consistent with J sc obtained from J–V curve Fig. 3(b). As a summary, several research on additive engineering of the CsPbI2Br PSCs in recent years are collected in Table S1, which shows the additives in this study realize a sufficient device performance. To understand the reasons of efficiency improvement, sorts of characterizations have been operated.

Generally speaking, the improvement of V oc is attributed to optimal energy level alignment, which effectively facilitates hole extraction while blocks the electrons to the anode. Ultraviolet photoemission spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were employed to investigate the band energy of the CsPbI2Br films. The UPS spectra are exhibited in Fig. 4 (a)-(b), from which the valence bands are extracted as 5.56 eV for the pristine film and 5.05 eV for CsPbI2Br film with NaCl + N-GQDs. Reflecting the calculated optical band-gap of 1.91 eV, the energy level diagrams of control and CsPbI2Br PSCs with NaCl + N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 4(c)–(d), wherein the work functions of ITO, Ag electrodes, and the energy band of SnO2 and P3HT are also illustrated [41,45]. The energy difference between the valence bands of the CsPbI2Br film with NaCl + N-GQDs and P3HT is remarkably smaller than that of the pristine film, which means the energy loss for hole transfer from the valence band of the perovskite to HTL is smaller.

DFT calculations (see Fig. S11) demonstrate that the up shift of the energy band is due to the substitution of Cl for Br [18], while the metallic N-GQDs play a role of electronic bridge to quickly transfer the photogenerated electrons to the SnO2 layer, avoiding the recombination of carriers at the grain boundaries. Fig. 4(e) shows a schematic of the band diagram of the perovskite/P3HT region and the density-of-states distribution of perovskite. N-GQDs passivate the defects at the grain boundary, meanwhile, they accelerate the transport of electrons at the grain boundary. As a result, the density of tail states near the valence and conduction bands suppressed to be lower with decreasing the defect density at mid gap by the addition engineering. Under the synergistic effect of the NaCl and N-GQDs, the recombination of carriers is greatly reduced and the collection of carriers is significantly increased, realizing optimized high-performance PSCs.

Additionally, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) and steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy measurements were operated on CsPbI2Br perovskite films spin-coated on ITO glasses with and without additives to characterize the carrier dynamics electronic passivation effects. In Fig. 5 (a), it is found that the PL intensity of incorporation film is enhanced compared to the pristine one, indicating the suppression of nonradiative carrier recombination [46]. Simultaneously, the slight blue-shift of PL peak for the incorporation film confirms a higher crystal quality with low defect density. Fig. 5(b) shows TRPL spectra of all films fitted by a biexponential decay function, and the corresponding fitted parameters are shown in Table S2. The average carrier lifetimes were calculated to be 9.41 ns for the film with NaCl + N-GQDs and 3.15 ns for the pristine film. The prolonged carrier lifetimes are associated with a decrease in electronic trap density. In order to explore the role of carrier extraction, the PL and TRPL spectrum for CsPbI2Br film spin-coated on SnO2 layer are shown in Fig. S12, one can be seen that faster and more efficient carrier extraction occurred in the film with NaCl + N-GQDs.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was characterized to further probe the carrier recombination of CsPbI2Br PSCs [47]. The equivalent circuit model and Nyquist plots are presented in Fig. 5(c) and Fig. S13. The recombination resistance (R rec) for the incorporation device is larger than the pristine device at a bias voltage of 1.0 V, resulted by the reduction of surface trap states. Besides, the space-charge limited current (SCLC) method is adopted to quantitatively assess the density of traps (n trap) in the CsPbI2Br layer by collecting dark I-V characteristics for the electron-only devices of ITO/SnO2/CsPbI2Br/PCBM/Ag structure and the hole-only devices of ITO/CsPbI2Br/P3HT/Ag structure. The n trap values were calculated using the equation: n trap = 2ε 0 εV TFL/eL [2], where ε 0 is the vacuum permittivity, ε is the relative dielectric constant of the perovskite material, V TFL is the trap filled limit voltage, e is the electron charge, and L is the thickness of the perovskite film [48]. As the SCLC curves shown in Fig. 5(d) and S14, the electron n trap are calculated to be 4.36 × 1015, 2.67 × 1015, 2.96 × 1015 and 1.68 × 1015 cm−3, while the hole n trap be 5.73 × 1015, 4.74 × 1015, 4.45 × 1015 and 2.77 × 1015 cm−3 for the pristine devices, devices with N-GQDs, devices with NaCl, and devices with NaCl + N-GQDs, respectively. The lower trap density benefits the reduction of nonradiative recombination and is consistent with the previous PL result.

Furthermore, the long-term stabilities of solar cell devices with and without binary additives were tested in the air with a humidity of 20%–30%. In Fig. 6 (a) and Fig. S15, the broken lines of time-dependent change of the normalized PCE, J sc, V oc, and FF for PSCs with and without NaCl + N-GQDs are displayed. The unencapsulated PSCs devices with binary additives maintain about 96% original PCE after storage in ambient air within 270 h. In striking contrast, the pristine devices only retain 86% initial PCE within 120 h under the same conditions. Ag diffused into the perovskite layer frequently in the pristine device, resulting in device damage after 120 h of measurement and loss of its photovoltaic characteristics [49]. Thanks to the improvement of film quality, the optimized device can still maintain good stability after 270 h. To understand the improved stability for PSCs with NaCl + N-GQDs, the water contact angle (WCA) was measured. As demonstrated in Fig. 6(b)(c), the CsPbI2Br layer with NaCl + N-GQDs has a bigger contact angle (50.79°) with the water droplet, thus exhibiting improved moisture resistance and stability, as compared with the pristine case (32.76°).

In summary, NaCl and N-GQDs as binary additives optimized the perovskite film quality and PSCs device performance. Experimental and DFT calculations results both demonstrated that NaCl and N-GQDs synergistically assisted the carrier transport in CsPbI2Br. Cl-doping aligns the energy level of CsPbI2Br, decreasing the energy barrier between perovskite and P3HT to promote hole transport and extraction. N-GQDs provide a role of electronic bridge to conduct electrons, avoiding the annihilation of electrons at Grain boundaries and surface. Moreover, the perovskite films with NaCl + N-GQDs realize the decreased trap state density, prolonging the carrier lifetime, and improve surface morphology. As a result, significantly improved V oc of 1.24 V and FF of 78% are achieved, lead to an improved PCE of 15.37% for CsPbI2Br PSCs with binary additives, while the PCE for the pristine PSCs is 12.52%, with a V oc of 1.16 V and an FF of 72%. This study proposed a simple and convenient binary additives optimization engineering for CsPbI2Br PSCs. However, defect-assisted recombination and interface-induced recombination result in the lower V oc than the previous literatures. To realize a better V oc, above mentioned issues need to be satisfied in the following study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61804070 and 11674138), the Science and Technology Projects of Gansu Province (No. 20JR10RA611 and 21JR7RA467), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. lzujbky-2021-60 and lzujbky-2021-sp54).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual interfacial design for efficient CsPbI2Br perovskite solar cells with improved photostability
Adv Mater

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qiulu Chen is currently a master candidate in School of Materials and Energy, Lanzhou University. Her research interest is perovskite solar cell.

Qiming Liu received his BS and MS degrees in Physics from Lanzhou University China, in 2008 and 2011, respectively. After that, he pursued his PhD degrees, under financial support of Japanese Government Scholarship, in Materials Science from Graduate School of Science and Engineering in Saitama University, Japan, in 2015. Then he stayed in Saitama University as a project researcher for two years. Currently, he is an associate professor in institute of electronic materials of Lanzhou University, China. His research focuses on the use of an integrated approach combining materials, interface, device, and process engineering to improve the energy devices especially in the field of photovoltaics and rechargeable batteries.

Junshuai Li is currently a Professor at School of Physical Science & Technology, Lanzhou University, China. Prior to holding this position, he performed the research on preparation of high quality PV materials and structures, design and fabrication of advanced Si nanostructure-based PV devices at Saitama University, Japan (2006.10–2008.09) and Nanyang Technological University, Singapore (2008.09–2012.03). Now his main research interest focuses on the optical and electrical behaviors in subwavelength semiconductor structures, and renewable energy devices.

Deyan He graduated from the Department of electronic chemistry, Graduate School of comprehensive science and engineering, Tokyo University of technology, Japan in March 1995, with a doctor's degree in engineering. From April 1995 to March 1996, he worked as a postdoctoral researcher at the University College of Tokyo Institute of technology in Japan. From September 1997 to July 1998, he worked as a senior visiting scholar in the Department of physics of Manitoba University in Canada. In December 2011, he was appointed to the senior secondary post, and in December 2019, he was selected to the secondary post of “Cuiying scholar” of Lanzhou University. He has long been engaged in the education and teaching of Physics related majors and the research and development of electronic film materials, semiconductor new energy materials and their devices.
